title: The First Thing My Savings Will Buy
subtitle: Journal prompts for building a personal case for saving
excerpt: Five journal prompts for connecting savings to something specific and wanted. Looking at what the savings are working toward makes the act of setting money aside feel purposeful rather than abstract.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 52 of The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798276631448).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to build motivation for saving, or when the savings habit has stalled.

content.text: Five journal prompts for looking at savings from the direction of what they make possible. Savings that have a named purpose are easier to maintain than savings that exist in the abstract. Set twenty minutes. Write without stopping.
New to journalling as practice? Read
What is my current relationship with saving?
Where does it sit right now: active, neglected, anxious, satisfying? One or two sentences.
How can saving and investing become acts of self-care rather than restriction?
Write what would need to shift in how you think about it for saving to feel like a kind thing you do for yourself.
The first thing my savings will buy is...
Name it specifically. Not 'a holiday' but 'a week on the Cornish coast in July.' Specificity makes the intention real.
What does that thing or experience make possible for me?
Write the feeling, not just the logistics.
What is one small step toward saving for this that I could take this week?
A concrete action: set up an automatic transfer, open a new savings account, move a specific amount.
Adapted from Day 52 of The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
